message.content: Adversaries may use Data Encoding to obfuscate command and control (C2) traffic, making it harder to detect. This technique involves encoding C2 information using standard systems like ASCII, Unicode, Base64, MIME, or other binary-to-text and character encoding methods. Encoding can also lead to data compression, such as with gzip. Examples include BADNEWS converting encrypted C2 data into hexadecimal and then Base64, H1N1 using an altered Base64 for obfuscation, Linux Rabbit sending payloads as encoded URL parameters, Mythic offering various encoding functions, and Ursnif embedding encoded data in HTTP URLs. The impact of this technique is significant as it complicates traffic analysis and detection efforts. Mitigation strategies include network traffic analysis to identify anomalies, implementing robust intrusion detection systems (IDS), and employing decryption and decoding tools to inspect encoded traffic. Regular updates to detection signatures and continuous monitoring are essential to counteract this technique effectively.
